task: I’ve got a post project that got messy fast: a few folders are mislabeled, some media notes don’t line up, and the client keeps shifting what they want included. Can you help me figure out a sane way to untangle it and get it back on track without making the next handoff worse?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest way is to stop treating it like one big mess and break it into three separate problems:

1. **project structure**
2. **source-of-truth notes**
3. **client scope / handoff expectations**

Here’s a sane way to untangle it without making the next handoff worse.

## 1) Freeze the current state first
Before changing anything, make a quick snapshot of what exists now.

- Duplicate the current project folder or save a dated backup
- Don’t rename everything at once
- Make a short “current state” note:
  - folder names as they are now
  - which media notes conflict
  - what the client has changed so far
  - what’s still unclear

This gives you a rollback point if something gets worse.

## 2) Create one source of truth
Pick one place where the real answers live.

Use a simple doc or spreadsheet with columns like:

- item / clip / folder
- current name
- intended name
- status
- notes
- owner / source
- needs client confirmation? yes/no

Then decide:
- which folder names are wrong
- which media notes are outdated
- which version is the latest approved one

If something conflicts, don’t guess — mark it as **needs confirmation**.

## 3) Clean up the folder logic before renaming
Fix the structure first, not the cosmetic names.

A practical order:
- top-level project folders
- media / exports / docs / delivery
- versioned subfolders
- archive / old / superseded

If folders are mislabeled, use a naming pattern that’s hard to break, like:
- `PROJECT_NAME_YYYY-MM-DD`
- `PROJECT_NAME_v01`, `v02`, `v03`
- `archive_old`
- `delivery_clientname`

If you already have a naming convention, keep it consistent rather than “improving” it midstream.

## 4) Reconcile the media notes against the actual files
Go file by file or batch by batch and compare:

- filename
- duration
- format / codec
- source date
- reel/card/tape info if relevant
- any burn-in or timecode references
- notes about selects, trims, or exclusions

If notes don’t line up, decide whether the note is:
- wrong
- incomplete
- referring to an older version
- actually describing a different asset

This is usually where a lot of hidden confusion lives.

## 5) Lock the client scope in writing
If the client keeps shifting what should be included, you need a small scope checkpoint.

Send a short message like:

> I want to make sure I’m updating the right version of this. Right now I have A, B, and C as the current included items. Can you confirm that’s the latest scope before I finalize the handoff?

If they keep changing it, summarize each change in one line:
- added
- removed
- replaced
- still pending

That way you’re not chasing a moving target without a record.

## 6) Separate “working files” from “handoff files”
To avoid making the next handoff worse, keep the cleanup contained.

Use:
- **working / cleanup** folder for your fixes
- **final / handoff** folder only for approved materials
- **archive** folder for old versions and superseded media

Don’t overwrite the handoff package while you’re still sorting things out.

## 7) Do a final handoff check
Before sending anything, verify:

- folder names are consistent
- latest version is obvious
- outdated files are clearly archived
- media notes match the actual assets
- client-approved inclusions are documented
- anything uncertain is flagged, not assumed

If there are still open questions, send a short note listing them instead of pretending it’s fully settled.

## 8) Keep the next handoff simpler
For the next round, make the handoff package easier to parse:

- one clear top-level folder
- dated/versioned exports
- a short readme or note file
- a change log
- a “confirmed by client” section

That prevents the same confusion from reappearing.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page cleanup checklist** or a **client update email** you can send right now.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is the right shape, but I’d want it tighter to actual post workflow. The big missing piece is a hard QC pass before any renaming or handoff, otherwise you just make the mess prettier.

What I’d add:
- make a locked backup first, obviously
- build a simple tracking sheet with columns for current name / intended name / source / status / client-approved
- separate factual mismatches from client-choice changes
- do not rename anything until you’ve matched the notes against the actual media
- keep a clean handoff folder totally separate from the working rescue folder

And for the client, I’d want language that forces a decision. Something like: “I’ve got two versions of the scope in play. I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are in.” No fluff.

If you want, I’d rather have this as a stricter step-by-step checklist for a real project folder, not the generic version.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a stricter, post-workflow version.

## Project rescue checklist: messy post folder / shifting scope

### 1) Freeze everything first
- Make a **locked backup** of the current project folder
- Do **not** rename, move, or delete anything yet
- If possible, duplicate the whole project to a **rescue copy** and work only there

### 2) Build a simple tracking sheet
Use one sheet for every folder / asset / note that’s in question.

Columns:
- **Current name**
- **Intended name**
- **Source**  
- **Status**  
- **Client-approved?**
- **Notes**

Use the sheet to track:
- mislabeled folders
- media notes that don’t match
- version confusion
- anything the client has changed

### 3) Run a hard QC pass before cleanup
Before any renaming, compare the notes against the actual media.

Check:
- filename
- duration
- format / codec
- reel/card/source info
- timecode / burn-in / slate info
- sync status
- mixed frame rates / retimes
- temp exports or old renders hiding in the sequence

Do **not** trust the notes until they’ve been matched to the files.

### 4) Separate the problems
Split issues into two buckets:

**Factual mismatches**
- wrong filename
- wrong duration
- wrong source
- notes that don’t match the media

**Client-choice changes**
- they changed what should be included
- they changed what version they want
- they changed the deliverable scope

That keeps you from fixing a client decision like it’s a metadata error.

### 5) Reconcile the media before renaming
- Match the notes to the actual files
- Flag anything uncertain as **needs confirmation**
- Only after QC is clean should you start renaming folders or files

### 6) Keep working rescue files separate from handoff files
Use three distinct areas:
- **working_rescue** — where you clean things up
- **handoff_clean** — only approved, final materials
- **archive_old** — superseded versions and anything replaced

Do not mix cleanup files with delivery files.

### 7) Rename only after QC is complete
Once the tracking sheet is clean:
- apply the naming convention consistently
- use dated / rev naming if needed
- keep old versions clearly archived
- don’t “improve” labels in a way that breaks traceability

### 8) Lock the final handoff package
Before delivery, verify:
- folder names are consistent
- latest approved version is obvious
- outdated files are out of the handoff folder
- notes match the media
- burn-ins / slates / timecode slugs are present if needed
- anything unresolved is flagged, not guessed

---

## Client message: force a decision

Here’s a plain version you can send:

> I’ve got two versions of the scope in play, so I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are in. Once I have that, I can finalize the handoff cleanly.

If you want it a little firmer:

> I’ve got two versions of the scope in play. I’m pausing final prep until you confirm which items are included, so I don’t hand off the wrong package.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist you can use on a real project folder** with the QC steps and handoff folder structure baked in.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, closer. But I’d still tighten two things:

1) the QC pass needs to happen **before** any structural cleanup, not just before renaming. I’d want picture/audio checks, version checks, and a quick compare against the notes so I’m not building on bad info.
2) the handoff package should have a final sign-off step — not just “looks consistent,” but a clear check that the client-approved scope matches what’s actually in the delivery folder.

If you want to make it actually usable, I’d want the checklist in the order I’d run it in the room:
- lock backup
- rescue copy
- QC against notes
- separate factual mismatches from scope changes
- confirm scope in writing
- only then rename / reorganize
- build handoff folder
- final QC on the handoff package

And the client note should be a little more exact about consequence. Something like:

> I’ve got two versions of the scope in play, so I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are included. I don’t want to finalize the wrong version.

That’s the kind of line I’d actually send.

So yeah — if you’ve got the one-page checklist version, make it run in that order and keep the QC gate hard.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tighter, real-world order with the QC gate up front.

## Post rescue checklist: messy folder / shifting scope

### 1) Lock the current state
- Make a **locked backup** of the current project folder
- Make a separate **rescue copy** to work in
- Do **not** rename, move, or delete anything yet

### 2) Run QC before any cleanup
Check the actual media against the notes before you touch structure.

Do a quick pass on:
- picture
- audio
- version numbers
- filenames
- durations
- source / reel / card info
- slate info / burn-ins / timecode slugs if relevant
- any mixed frame rates, retimes, or odd timeline behavior

Goal: confirm what is actually there before you start organizing it.

### 3) Separate the problems
Sort issues into two buckets:

**Factual mismatches**
- mislabeled folders
- wrong filenames
- notes that don’t match the media
- outdated version references

**Scope changes**
- client added or removed items
- client changed which version is approved
- deliverable content shifted

Don’t treat a client decision like a metadata error.

### 4) Build a tracking sheet
Use one simple sheet with columns like:
- current name
- intended name
- source
- status
- client-approved
- notes

Track every item that’s unclear or inconsistent.

### 5) Confirm scope in writing
If the client has two versions of the scope floating around, stop and force a decision.

Use something like:

> I’ve got two versions of the scope in play, so I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are included. I don’t want to finalize the wrong version.

### 6) Only then clean up structure
Once QC is done and scope is confirmed:
- rename folders/files
- reorganize the project
- keep naming consistent
- preserve traceability to the original assets

### 7) Build the handoff package separately
Keep a clean delivery folder totally separate from the rescue/work folder.

Suggested structure:
- `working_rescue`
- `handoff_clean`
- `archive_old`

Don’t mix cleanup files with delivery files.

### 8) Final QC on the handoff package
Before delivery, confirm:
- client-approved scope matches what’s in the folder
- latest version is obvious
- no outdated files are included
- notes match the media
- required burn-ins / slates / timecode are present
- nothing unresolved is being guessed at

### 9) Final sign-off
Do one last check that the delivery folder and the approved scope match exactly.

If anything is still unclear, flag it before handoff — don’t bury it in the package.

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean one-page checklist** with checkboxes you can actually use during a rescue pass.

user_simulator.text:
Closer, but I’d tighten the final sign-off so it’s not just a note at the end. That needs to be its own hard gate — like, no delivery until the approved scope sheet and the contents of the handoff folder match line by line.

Also I’d want the QC step to explicitly include a quick pass on any exports or renders already sitting in the project, because that’s where stale stuff sneaks back in. Seen that happen. Ugly.

If you’re doing the one-page version, I’d want it to read in this order:
- lock backup
- rescue copy
- QC against notes
- separate factual mismatches from scope changes
- confirm scope in writing
- cleanup / rename
- build handoff folder
- final QC on handoff folder
- final sign-off against approved scope

And keep the client line exactly like this or close:

> I’ve got two versions of the scope in play, so I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are included. I don’t want to finalize the wrong version.

That’s usable. Just needs the sign-off made more explicit, not implied.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the tightened one-page version with the sign-off as a hard gate and the render/export check included.

## Post rescue checklist: messy folder / shifting scope

1. **Lock backup**
   - Make a locked backup of the current project folder.

2. **Rescue copy**
   - Work only from a separate rescue copy.
   - Do not rename, move, or delete anything in the original.

3. **QC against notes**
   - Compare the actual media to the notes before any cleanup.
   - Check:
     - picture
     - audio
     - version numbers
     - filenames
     - durations
     - source / reel / card info
     - slate info / burn-ins / timecode slugs if relevant
     - mixed frame rates / retimes
     - any exports or renders already sitting in the project

4. **Separate factual mismatches from scope changes**
   - Factual mismatches: mislabeled folders, wrong filenames, notes that don’t match media, stale exports/renders.
   - Scope changes: client-added or removed items, changed approvals, revised deliverable content.

5. **Confirm scope in writing**
   - Stop and get the client to confirm the current scope before cleanup continues.

   Client note:
   > I’ve got two versions of the scope in play, so I’m holding final prep until you confirm which items are included. I don’t want to finalize the wrong version.

6. **Cleanup / rename**
   - Only after QC and scope confirmation:
     - rename folders/files
     - reorganize structure
     - keep traceability intact

7. **Build handoff folder**
   - Keep a clean delivery folder separate from the rescue/work folder.
   - Don’t mix cleanup files with delivery files.

8. **Final QC on handoff folder**
   - Check that the handoff folder contains only approved materials.
   - Verify:
     - latest version is obvious
     - no stale exports/renders slipped back in
     - notes match the media
     - required burn-ins / slates / timecode are present
     - nothing unresolved is being guessed at

9. **Final sign-off against approved scope**
   - Compare the approved scope sheet line by line against the contents of the handoff folder.
   - No delivery until they match exactly.
   - If there’s any mismatch, stop and resolve it first.

If you want, I can turn this into a checkbox format you can paste straight into a project doc.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — that’s it. The sign-off gate is explicit now, and the export/render check being inside QC is the right place for it. This is usable.

The line about no delivery until the approved scope sheet matches the handoff folder line by line is exactly the part that keeps people from hand-waving the last step. That’s the thing I’d actually want in the room.

If you want to make it even more practical, the only extra I’d add is a tiny status column in the sheet for the sign-off pass itself — something like pending / matched / blocked. But as-is, this is solid.
